explanation.title: How CSS Works and How to Target Elements with Selectors
explanation.content:
# What is CSS? If HTML is the skeleton of a webpage, **CSS** (Cascading Style Sheets) is the skin, clothes, and cosmetics. It controls everything visual: colours, fonts, spacing, sizes, animations, and layout. CSS is a separate language from HTML. You write rules that say: find these elements, then apply these styles. ## Three Ways to Add CSS **1. Inline** — directly on one element (least maintainable): ```html

Red text

``` **2. Internal** — inside a ` ``` **3. External** — a separate `.css` file (recommended for all real projects): ```html ``` ## CSS Rule Anatomy ```css selector { property: value; property: value; } ``` - **Selector** — which HTML elements to style - **Property** — what to change (color, font-size, margin…) - **Value** — the new setting - Each property-value pair ends with a semicolon `;` ## The Core Selectors | Selector | Syntax | Targets | |---|---|---| | Element | `p` | All `

` elements | | Class | `.card` | All elements with `class="card"` | | ID | `#hero` | The one element with `id="hero"` | | Group | `h1, h2, h3` | All h1, h2, and h3 elements | | Descendant | `nav a` | All `` inside a `

` | | Child | `ul > li` | Direct `
  • ` children of `
      ` | | Pseudo-class | `a:hover` | An `` when the mouse is over it | ## The Cascade and Specificity "Cascading" in CSS means that when multiple rules target the same element, the winner is determined by **specificity** (how specific the selector is): - ID selectors win over class selectors - Class selectors win over element selectors - Later rules beat earlier rules when specificity is equal - `!important` overrides everything (use sparingly) In the code example below, you will see element, class, ID, and pseudo-class selectors all applied to the same page.

codeExample.code:

codeExample.explanation:
- `p { ... }` — element selector; applies to every `

` on the page - `.highlight { ... }` — class selector; the dot prefix targets elements with that class name - `#hero { ... }` — ID selector; the hash prefix targets the unique element with that id - `h2, h3 { ... }` — group selector; comma separates multiple targets sharing the same rules - `nav a { ... }` — descendant selector; styles only `` elements that are inside `

` - `nav a:hover { ... }` — pseudo-class; applies only when the cursor is positioned over the link

exercise.title: Style a Profile Card with Selectors
exercise.instructions:
Create an HTML page with a CSS style block that uses at least four different selector types. The page must have: an element selector styling all p tags, a class selector for a class named 'card' with a border and padding, an ID selector for id='headline' with a green color, and a pseudo-class hover style on anchor tags. Include matching HTML elements in the body to demonstrate each selector.

Expected: page with element, class, ID, and pseudo-class selectors all applied.

hints: 💡 A class selector starts with a dot: .card { border: 1px solid #ccc; padding: 16px; }
💡 An ID selector starts with a hash: #headline { color: #22c55e; }
💡 A hover pseudo-class is written as a:hover { text-decoration: underline; } inside the style block.

questions.question: Which CSS selector targets an element with the attribute class="card"?
questions.explanation: Class selectors in CSS start with a dot (period): .card targets all elements that have class="card" in their HTML. The hash symbol (#) is used for ID selectors. Writing just card without a prefix would target elements with that tag name, which is only valid for actual HTML elements like p or h1.

questions.question: What does a CSS pseudo-class like :hover do?
options: It creates a fake element that appears on hover
It applies styles only when the element is in a specific state
It overrides all other CSS rules
questions.explanation: A pseudo-class applies styles to an element only when it is in a particular state. a:hover applies styles when the cursor is over a link. Other common pseudo-classes include :focus (when an input is selected), :active (when a button is clicked), and :nth-child(n) (targeting elements by position).

questions.question: When two CSS rules target the same element with equal specificity, which one wins?
options: The first rule defined always wins
The later rule defined in the stylesheet wins
Both rules are applied and merge their values
questions.explanation: When two rules have identical specificity, the cascade applies and the rule that appears later in the stylesheet wins. This is the 'cascading' part of Cascading Style Sheets. For properties that do not conflict, both rules apply. Understanding this order is essential for debugging unexpected CSS behaviour.
